chunk:
CHUNK:006/021
\u201cNaamik.\u201d Uiluiit tapped his cheek and said, \u201cI ran through them, got caught on a line, and snagged my cheek.\u201d He shrugged when I wrinkled my nose in sympathy. \u201cTwenty-seven stitches.\u201d I leaned forward for a closer look at Uiluiit\u2019s scar and then shook my head, not quite believing it. The scar wasn\u2019t that long, and I said as much, falling swiftly into the punchline Uiluiit had set up. \u201cTwelve for me,\u201d he said. \u201cAnd fifteen for Makkoq.\u201d Uiluiit shrugged again and said, \u201cHe stopped to laugh. And then, teasing me, he ran circles around the fish rack, and straight into a line the fisherman had stretched between two racks.\u201d Uiluiit raised his arms like a B movie mummy, adding, \u201cHe ran straight into it, and he struggled, and it he got snagged.\u201d Uiluiit paused for a breath before he continued. \u201cWe spent a few hours together in the hospital, both getting stitches, and then ice cream after that.\u201d \u201cFlavour?\u201d I said, thinking it was important. \u201cChocolate,\u201d Uiluiit said. Uiluiit chuckled softly for a second or two, and then, with another breath, he looked up to catch my eye, and I could feel a subtle shift in the air between us. \u201cBut that\u2019s not why I waited for you,\u201d he said. \u201cAnd you don\u2019t need a ride,\u201d I said, with a nod at the dinghy Gaba had rented. \u201cI don\u2019t.\u201d I waited another few seconds, thinking that Arnet might have said something to Uiluiit, and that my break would soon be over because Uiluiit had given me a nugget of information that could change the nature of our babysitting operation. I just didn\u2019t know why Uiluiit couldn\u2019t have said it directly to Gaba when he had the chance. Or perhaps I had met one of the few male police officers in Greenland who was not under Sergeant Alatak\u2019s spell. That in itself was interesting enough, but failed in comparison to what Uiluiit wanted to tell me. \u201cYou knew Constable Innaaq Paniula?\u201d he said. \u201cYes.\u201d I swallowed, and said nothing more, wondering just how much Uiluiit knew about what happened the last time I was in the area. The rumour mill among the Greenland police was an effective one, with gossip being a tradable commodity, especially for officers assigned to the more remote settlements where a lone constable might feel very isolated indeed. I understood that my exploits as a young constable had not gone unnoticed and didn\u2019t doubt Uiluiit had heard a story or two, especially given that he was the local constable for the area. But he still surprised me when he told me what he had to say. \u201cThere is a cabin just off the path to Tasiusaq,\u201d he said. \u201cI was there about two months ago. Some tourists had taken shelter inside when one of them felt ill.\u201d Uiluiit shrugged and said something about diabetes and helicopters, and a lot of paperwork. And then he looked me in the eye, took a breath, and said, \u201cThere is an old wooden desk in the cabin. Inside the desk is a drawer.\u201d \u201cYes,\u201d I said, aware that I needed to breathe once in a while, but too curious to risk losing a single word of what Uiluiit had to say. \u201cInside the drawer is a diary. It used to belong to Constable Paniula.\u201d I nodded; eyes fixed on Uiluiit. \u201cI only read a few lines,\u201d he said. \u201cBut your name was on one of them.\u201d \u201cMy name?\u201d \u201cAap,\u201d he said. \u201cAnd you left it there?\u201d \u201cI was busy,\u201d Uiluiit said, and I nodded. \u201cOf course.\u201d \u201cBut now\u2026\u201d Uiluiit pointed at the shore and the red and blue wooden houses of Qassiarsuk in the near distance. \u201cNow you\u2019re on a break.\u201d \u201cYes,\u201d I said. \u201cThen I think you should go to the cabin, Constable. I think you should read Paniula\u2019s diary.\u201d \u201cYes,\u201d I said, because I thought so too. The thought of catching a diamond smuggler \u2013 one of America\u2019s Most Wanted criminals \u2013 had been interesting. And, while discovering the truth behind Rachael\u2019s bruised cheek still occupied a tiny part of my mind, there was nothing, nothing that burned more insistently in my mind than the urge to get my hands on Paniula\u2019s diary. \u201cYou\u2019d better get going,\u201d Uiluiit said. \u201cThe cabin is about halfway along the path. There and back is about seven kilometres.\u201d \u201cYes,\u201d I said, nodding once before pushing off the railing and heading for the dinghy. \u201cI hope it\u2019s worth it,\u201d Uiluiit said as I climbed into the dinghy and fiddled with the outboard motor. I looked up and realised he knew that it was, and I guessed he had read more than just a few lines. \u201cGood luck, Constable,\u201d Uiluiit said, waving once as I started the motor. \n 5 There was a bit of spray splashing over the bow, but it had nothing to do with the wind, and the waves were of my own making. Gaba had said something about popping into Qassiarsuk to do the cultural thing and had suggested I take a couple of hours to do just that before returning to the ship to rest, and, I guessed, to grab something to eat before taking my watch. But I was already way ahead of him, dropping anything that might be confused as cultural, and plotting a brisk hike to the cabin. I figured I had just enough time to grab the diary, jog back to the dinghy, and sail back to the ship within three hours, leaving just enough time for a sandwich and a snooze before pounding caffeine in any shape or form I could get my hands on until the Americans arrived. It was a good plan. And, if I stuck to the plan, if I really focused on it, then I could stop my brain speculating on this or wondering about that, until I had the diary in my hands and discovered what it was Constable Uiluiit thought I might want to know.
